structured.summary: This paper proposes P-Adapters, lightweight models inserted between the embedding layer and first attention layer of frozen LLMs, to robustly extract factual information from language models given diverse natural language prompts. The approach improves precision and consistency over baselines without requiring additional annotations, and is evaluated on multiple out-of-distribution settings.

strengths.point: The paper addresses the important problem of prompt variability affecting factual extraction from LLMs, proposing a novel and lightweight P-Adapter architecture that does not require fine-tuning the LLM or additional annotations at inference time.
strengths.evidence: Introduction and Section 4.2 describe the P-Adapter design and motivation for requiring only (prompt, object) pairs at training and no extra annotations at inference.

strengths.point: Comprehensive evaluation on multiple out-of-distribution settings (OOD Prompts, OOD Objects, OOD Keyboard Errors) demonstrates robustness of P-Adapters beyond in-domain data.
strengths.evidence: Section 4.1 details the four evaluation settings; Section 5 discusses results showing consistent improvements across these challenging OOD scenarios.

strengths.point: The paper provides insightful ablations showing the importance of preserving the subject token embeddings in the prompt for successful factual extraction.
strengths.evidence: Section 5 discusses experiments replacing subject and [MASK] embeddings, demonstrating that keeping subject embeddings unmodified significantly improves precision.

strengths.point: Comparison with Mixture of Experts (MoE) and Oracle models highlights the trade-off between requiring additional annotations and performance gains, positioning P-Adapters as a practical alternative.
strengths.evidence: Section 4.2 and Section 5 compare P-Adapters with MoE and Oracle models, showing P-Adapters perform comparably without needing relation or subject annotations.

strengths.point: The paper clearly defines terminology distinguishing natural language prompts, continuous prompts, and embeddings, aiding reader understanding of the method.
strengths.evidence: Section 3 Terminology provides precise definitions of prompt types and embeddings.

strengths.point: The proposed interpolation technique between P-Adapter outputs and original embeddings to improve performance without extra annotations is a clever and practical solution.
strengths.evidence: Section 5 describes the interpolation approach with hyperparameter α and shows improved results without needing subject token indices.

strengths.point: The paper situates its contributions well within related work on prompting and factual knowledge extraction, acknowledging limitations of prior approaches.
strengths.evidence: Section 2 Related Work discusses prior prompting methods, challenges with consistency, and overfitting to object distributions.

weaknesses.point: The paper lacks a detailed analysis of why the P-Adapters still achieve relatively low absolute precision (around 50% or less) even in the best case, limiting practical utility.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 6 Discussion notes that the Oracle achieves only ~50% precision on ID objects and ~25% on OOD objects, but does not deeply analyze causes or potential remedies.

weaknesses.point: The evaluation focuses exclusively on masked language models (BERT, RoBERTa) and does not assess applicability to autoregressive LLMs, limiting generalizability claims.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4.2 specifies experiments only on BERT Base, BERT Large, and RoBERTa Large; no experiments on GPT-style or decoder-only models are presented.

weaknesses.point: The paper does not provide ablations or analysis on the impact of prompt length or complexity on P-Adapter performance, which could affect real-world usage.
weaknesses.evidence: While Section 4.1 mentions prompt template pools, no experiments vary prompt length or syntactic complexity to assess robustness.

weaknesses.point: The MoE model uses a single predicted relation to select an expert prompt rather than a weighted mixture, which is a simplification that may limit its performance and comparison fairness.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4.2 states the MoE uses a single prompt from the predicted relation instead of a weighted combination, but does not justify this design choice or explore alternatives.

weaknesses.point: The paper does not report runtime or computational overhead introduced by P-Adapters, leaving unclear their efficiency and scalability in practice.
weaknesses.evidence: No mention of inference speed, parameter counts, or memory overhead is found in Sections 4 or 5.

weaknesses.point: The method requires knowledge of the [MASK] token position in the prompt, but the paper does not discuss how this requirement generalizes to more complex or multi-mask prompts.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 5 discusses preserving the [MASK] embedding but does not address scenarios with multiple masks or different masking strategies.

weaknesses.point: The paper does not provide qualitative examples of prompts and model outputs, which would help illustrate the nature of errors and improvements.
weaknesses.evidence: No example predictions or failure cases are shown in the main text or appendix.

weaknesses.point: The training details are somewhat sparse, lacking information on training time, hardware used, or convergence behavior, which hinders reproducibility.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4.2 briefly lists hyperparameters but omits details on epochs, training duration, or computational resources.

weaknesses.point: The interpolation hyperparameter α is selected empirically without discussion of sensitivity or how it might be tuned in practice.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 5 reports testing α values but does not analyze robustness to this choice or provide guidance for new datasets.

weaknesses.point: The paper does not discuss potential ethical risks of improved factual extraction, such as amplifying biases or privacy concerns beyond a brief ethics statement.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 8 Ethics Statement mentions risks but does not analyze or propose mitigation strategies.

questions.question: Have the authors considered applying P-Adapters to autoregressive LLMs (e.g., GPT-style models), and if so, how might the method adapt to such architectures?
questions.motivation: Demonstrating applicability beyond masked LMs would strengthen claims of generalizability and practical impact.

questions.question: Can the authors provide qualitative examples of prompts where P-Adapters significantly improve predictions or fail, to better understand model behavior?
questions.motivation: Examples would clarify the nature of improvements and guide future refinements.

questions.question: How sensitive is the interpolation hyperparameter α to dataset or prompt variations, and can it be tuned without labeled data?
questions.motivation: Understanding α’s robustness is important for deploying P-Adapters in new domains without extensive tuning.

questions.question: What is the computational overhead of P-Adapters at inference time compared to baseline prompting, and is it feasible for real-time applications?
Could the authors elaborate on how P-Adapters handle prompts with multiple [MASK] tokens or more complex query structures?
questions.motivation: Many real-world queries may involve multiple missing tokens; understanding limitations here is important.

questions.question: Is it possible to extend P-Adapters to incorporate external knowledge or context dynamically, to improve factual accuracy beyond pretraining?
questions.motivation: Combining P-Adapters with retrieval or knowledge integration could address current precision limitations.
